critic.verdict: A decent Easy chart with creative hold patterns, but marred by awkward crossovers, forced double-steps, and overly complex syncopations for beginners.

issues.problem: This note is placed on an off-beat (3.5 beats into the intro), which creates an unintuitive rhythm for an Easy chart.
The pattern D -> U -> L -> D forces either a spin/crossover or awkward double-stepping at 160 BPM, which is too complex for an Easy player.
A syncopated off-beat note (dotted quarter rhythm) right before the drop disrupts the player's momentum and feel of the beat.
A sudden eighth-note pickup (gallop) at 160 BPM is highly unexpected and difficult for players at this difficulty level.
The sequence D -> L -> R -> U -> D has poor flow, requiring unnatural foot transitions and crossovers.

critic.verdict: A well-synced, energetic chart that matches the robotic vibe of the track, but suffers from repetitive patterning and a few awkward high-speed transitions.

issues.problem: The constant repetition of JUMP LR -> tap U -> tap D -> tap L feels highly rigid and gets physically repetitive to play, missing opportunities for more varied choreography.
A 16th-note gallop from U to D at 160 BPM is extremely uncomfortable, requiring a fast double-step or slide across opposite panels which is too punishing for a Medium-level chart.
Tapping U and immediately transitioning into a JUMP LU creates an awkward double-tap on U during a jump, causing a hitch in player momentum.
Releasing HOLD U while hitting tap D at 31.791, followed by a 16th-note tap R at 31.884, requires a highly technical foot-swap/weight transfer that exceeds the expected difficulty.

critic.verdict: Rhythmically accurate and well-synced to the hard dance beats, but contains physically impossible 3-way holds and unplayable one-foot 16th streams on pad.

issues.problem: 3-panel overlapping holds (e.g. D+R+U, D+L+U) are present, which are impossible to hold on pad without hands or extreme bracket play, which doesn't fit a standard hard dance single chart.

While U is held, the chart requires a 16th-note stream (L -> R -> D) to be played. Since one foot is locking U, the other foot has to tap 16th notes across opposite panels (L to R to D) at 160 BPM, which is physically unplayable.
The stream pattern (D -> R -> L -> U -> D -> L) results in extremely awkward double-steps or forced 180-degree spins that disrupt the high-energy flow of the section.
While D is held, there is a 16th-note transition from U to R with the free foot, which is too fast for a single-foot slide at this tempo.
